note:
γ batch input. 100 clusters.

For each cluster, output a verdict in strict JSON:
  {"cluster_hash": "<hash>", "word": "<word>", "decision": "merge|split|unsure", "confidence": 0.0-1.0, "reasoning": "1 sentence", "examples_substitutable_pct": 0-100, "merged_text": "..." | null}

Save as: data/simplify_diff/gamma_batch_verdicts.json

GAMMA_SYSTEM_PROMPT:
You are an English vocabulary teacher reviewing 2-3 senses of a single word for an IELTS learner's flashcard.

YOUR RULE (the user's "80-90% rule"):
Merge the senses ONLY IF a single definition lets the learner understand AT LEAST 80-90% of all examples across the senses. Otherwise SPLIT.

OUTPUT FORMAT (strict JSON, no prose):
{
  "decision": "merge" | "split" | "unsure",
  "confidence": 0.0-1.0,
  "reasoning": "1 sentence",
  "examples_substitutable_pct": 0-100,
  "merged_text": "single definition" | null
}

DECISION RULES:
- merge:    1 def covers >=80% examples, learner doesn't need separate cards
- split:    senses are too distinct, need 2+ cards
- unsure:   borderline 70-80%, default to SPLIT in production (conservative)

NEVER merge senses that are clearly different POS, register, or context (e.g. "go" verb vs "go" noun).

senses.text: to care for young children or animals until they are fully grown
examples: She reared a family of five on her own.
Lions usually manage to rear about half the number of cubs born to them.

senses.text: to keep and breed (= produce young from) animals or birds, for example on a farm
examples: to rear cattle

senses.text: having knowledge or skill in a particular job or activity
examples: an experienced player/teacher
She's highly experienced in software development.
He's very experienced in looking after animals.

senses.text: having knowledge as a result of doing something for a long time, or having had a lot of different experiences
examples: She's very young and not very experienced.
an experienced traveller (= somebody who has travelled a lot)
She was more mature, more experienced.

senses.text: the air that you take into your lungs and send out again
examples: His breath smelt of garlic.
bad breath (= that smells bad)
She was very short of breath (= had difficulty breathing).
Patients develop a cough and shortness of breath.
There was a sharp intake of breath in the courtroom.

senses.text: an amount of air that enters the lungs at one time
examples: He recited the whole poem in one breath.

senses.text: a chemical element. Carbon is found in all living things, existing in a pure state as diamond and graphite.
examples: carbon fibre
iron with a high carbon content

senses.text: used when referring to the gas carbon dioxide in terms of the effect it has on the earth’s climate in causing global warming
examples: carbon emissions/levels/taxes

senses.text: the act or process of combining two or more things so that they work together
examples: The aim is to promote closer economic integration.
His music is an integration of tradition and new technology.

senses.text: the act or process of mixing people who have previously been separated, usually because of colour, race, religion, etc.
examples: racial integration in schools
measures to promote the social integration of people with learning difficulties
the use of sport to facilitate the integration of child refugees into society

senses.text: dishonest or illegal behaviour, especially of people in authority
examples: allegations of bribery and corruption
The new district attorney has promised to fight police corruption.

senses.text: the act or effect of making somebody change from moral to immoral standards of behaviour
examples: He claimed that sex and violence on TV led to the corruption of young people.

senses.text: a person who is admired by many people for doing something brave or good
examples: a war hero (= somebody who was very brave during a war)
a hero of the First World War
one of the country’s national heroes
His charity work has made him something of a local hero.
The Olympic team were given a hero's welcome on their return home.

senses.text: the main character in a story, novel, film, etc., especially the main male character
examples: The hero of the novel is a ten-year old boy.
action heroes like Chris Hemsworth

senses.text: a person that you admire because of a particular quality or skill that they have
examples: my childhood hero
He became a hero to millions for his decision to oppose the government's reforms.

senses.text: a mistake in something that means that it is not correct or does not work correctly
examples: The argument is full of fundamental flaws.
The report reveals fatal flaws in security at the airport.
There is still a basic flaw in your selection procedure.

senses.text: a weakness in somebody’s character
examples: There is always a flaw in the character of a tragic hero.

senses.text: the time of year when the crops are gathered in on a farm, etc.; the act of cutting and gathering crops
examples: harvest time
Farmers are extremely busy during the harvest.

senses.text: the crops, or the amount of crops, cut and gathered
examples: the grain harvest
a good/bad harvest (= a lot of crops or few crops)
The appeal produced a rich harvest of blankets, medicines and clothing.
Plum growers say they are expecting a bumper harvest (= a large amount of produce) this year.

senses.text: an area of a country or town, especially one that has particular features
examples: the City of London’s financial district
Every city has its central business district.
The house is in a historic district.
Milan's most fashionable shopping district
a poor district of the city

senses.text: one of the areas that a country, town or state is divided into for purposes of organization, with official boundaries (= borders)
examples: a tax district
a village in the Darjeeling district
a district judge
the district health authority

senses.text: an unusual, exciting or dangerous experience, journey or series of events
examples: her adventures travelling in Africa
adventure stories
Four members of our staff have embarked on the adventure of a lifetime.
They fly around in their rocket ship and go on exciting adventures.
When you're a child, life is one big adventure.

senses.text: the quality of being excited and willing to take risks, try new ideas, etc.
examples: a sense/spirit of adventure
I set out across the country looking for adventure.
the adventure travel industry
Travelling by train has always been associated with romance and adventure.

senses.text: the official home of a king, queen, president, etc.
examples: Buckingham Palace
the royal/presidential palace
He spent his early days at the Palace of Versailles.
the palace gates/grounds/walls

senses.text: any large impressive house
examples: The Old Town has a whole collection of churches, palaces and mosques.
Even this small house was a veritable palace compared to his tent.

senses.text: a public celebration of a special day or event, usually with bands in the streets and decorated vehicles
examples: the Lord Mayor’s parade
St Patrick’s Day parade in New York

senses.text: a formal occasion when soldiers march or stand in lines so that they can be inspected (= looked at and approved) by their officers or other important people
examples: a military parade
They held a parade to mark the soldiers' return.
They stood as straight as soldiers on parade.
The latest software will be on parade at the exhibition.

senses.text: to give somebody support, courage or hope
examples: We were greatly encouraged by the positive response of the public.
She encouraged many young writers and artists.
My parents have always encouraged me in my choice of career.
‘You're doing fine,’ he encouraged them.

senses.text: to persuade somebody to do something by making it easier for them and making them believe it is a good thing to do
examples: Banks actively encouraged people to borrow money.
I strongly encourage everyone to go and see this important film.
Speaking your mind is highly encouraged at these sessions.

senses.text: to make something more likely to happen or develop
examples: There is a clear case for spending public money on encouraging participation in sport.
to encourage development/investment/growth
The government should encourage the use of renewable energy sources.
They claim that some computer games encourage violent behaviour in young children.
Music and lighting are used to encourage shoppers to buy more.

senses.text: the way a surface, substance or piece of cloth feels when you touch it, for example how rough, smooth, hard or soft it is
examples: the soft texture of velvet
She uses a variety of different colours and textures in her wall hangings.
The method used will vary with the soil texture and climate.

senses.text: the way food or drink tastes or feels in your mouth, for example whether it is rough, smooth, light, heavy, etc.
examples: The two cheeses were very different in both taste and texture.

senses.text: waste gases that come out of a vehicle, an engine or a machine
examples: car exhaust fumes/emissions

senses.text: the system in a vehicle through which exhaust gases come out
examples: My car needs a new exhaust.
pollution from car exhausts

senses.text: having the job or duty of doing something or taking care of somebody/something, so that you may be blamed if something goes wrong
examples: Mike is responsible for designing the entire project.
Even where parents no longer live together, they each continue to be responsible for their children.
The management team is directly responsible for the day-to-day operations of the company.
I'd like to talk to whoever is responsible here.

senses.text: being able to be blamed for something
examples: Who's responsible for this mess?
He is mentally ill and cannot be held responsible for his actions.
We will ensure that anyone found responsible for the accident is held accountable.
Everything will be done to bring those responsible to justice.
You mustn't feel responsible in any way.

senses.text: the place where two or more roads or railway lines meet
examples: It was near the junction of City Road and Old Street.
Come off the motorway at junction 6.
The most complicated motorway interchange in Britain is known as Spaghetti Junction.
In the 19th century the town became an important railway junction.

senses.text: a place where two or more cables, rivers or other things meet or are joined
examples: a telephone junction box
the junction between nerves and muscles

senses.text: a person who is the official head of a country or region that is governed by another country
examples: the former governor of the colony
He was confirmed as the provincial governor for another five-year term.

senses.text: a person who is chosen to be in charge of the government of a state in the US
examples: the governor of Arizona
the Arizona governor
Governor Bev Perdue

senses.text: the way that somebody deals with or treats a situation, a person, an animal, etc.
examples: I was impressed by his handling of the affair.
This horse needs patient handling.
Why is he being so polite, as if I need tactful handling?

senses.text: the action of touching, feeling or holding something with your hands
examples: toys that can stand up to rough handling
In the manufacturing industries heavy materials handling is often done by robots.
hygienic food handling practice

senses.text: a series of letters, numbers, etc. that you must type into a computer or computer system in order to be able to use it
examples: Enter a username and password to get into the system.

senses.text: a secret word or phrase that you need to know in order to be allowed into a place
examples: You must give the password before they'll let you in.

senses.text: houses, flats, etc. that people live in, especially when referring to their type, price or condition
examples: rental/student housing
temporary/permanent housing
low-cost/cheap housing
There is an urgent need to build more affordable housing.
We must find a way to solve the city's housing crisis.

senses.text: the job of providing houses, flats, etc. for people to live in
examples: Tenants may only keep pets with the written consent of the housing department.
They discussed how the council's housing policy could be changed to deal with the growing housing crisis.

senses.text: able to think quickly; quick to notice things
examples: Suddenly he found himself awake and fully alert.
Two alert scientists spotted the mistake.
Try to stay alert while driving at night.
an alert mind
mentally alert

senses.text: aware of something, especially a problem or danger
examples: We must be alert to the possibility of danger.
She was alert to the slightest sound.

senses.text: to build something
examples: The church was erected in 1582.

senses.text: to put something in position so that it stands upright
examples: Police had to erect barriers to keep crowds back.
to erect a tent

senses.text: a room in which there is a bath, a washbasin and often a toilet
examples: Go and wash your hands in the bathroom.
The master bedroom also has an en suite bathroom.
The family bathroom has a washbasin, toilet, and bath with shower attachment.
She stared at herself in the bathroom mirror.

senses.text: a room in which there is a toilet, a sink and sometimes a bath or shower
examples: I have to go to the bathroom (= use the toilet).
Where's the bathroom? (= for example in a restaurant)
We were allowed to stop occasionally for bathroom breaks.
Bill asked if he could use the bathroom.

senses.text: an official document that may be used to prove that the facts it states are true
examples: a birth/marriage/death certificate
a certificate of motor insurance

senses.text: an official document proving that you have completed a course of study or passed an exam; a qualification obtained after a course of study or an exam
examples: a Postgraduate Certificate in Education (= a British qualification for teachers)
A certificate of completion will be issued to all who complete the course.

senses.text: costing a lot of money, especially more than you want to pay
examples: Buying new furniture may prove too costly.

senses.text: causing problems or the loss of something
examples: a costly mistake/failure
Mining can be costly in terms of lives (= too many people can die).

senses.text: the act of taking somebody/something away from a particular place
examples: Clearance of the site required the removal of a number of trees.
the removal of a tumour
Allegations of abuse led to the removal of several children from their families.

senses.text: the act of getting rid of something
examples: stain removal
the removal of trade barriers

senses.text: for which there is a fair and acceptable reason
examples: a legitimate grievance
It seemed a perfectly legitimate question.
Politicians are legitimate targets for satire.

senses.text: allowed and acceptable according to the law
examples: the legitimate government of the country
Is his business strictly legitimate?
The legitimate government was reinstated after the uprising.

senses.text: to exist or be very common at a particular time or in a particular place
examples: We were horrified at the conditions prevailing in local prisons.
Those beliefs still prevail among certain social groups.

senses.text: to be accepted, especially after a struggle or an argument
examples: Justice will prevail over tyranny.
Fortunately, common sense prevailed.

senses.text: a hollow space in something solid or in the surface of something
examples: He dug a deep hole in the garden.
The bomb blew a huge hole in the ground.
Water had collected in the holes in the road.
She drilled a small hole in the wall.

senses.text: a space or opening that goes all the way through something
examples: We drilled a hole through the wall.
The car was riddled with bullet holes.
The old blankets were full of holes.
There were holes in the knees of his trousers.
There's a gaping hole in the ceiling and rain is pouring onto my desk.

senses.text: the quality of being expressed clearly
examples: a lack of clarity in the law
The brilliant clarity of his argument could not be faulted.

senses.text: the ability to think about or understand something clearly
examples: clarity of thought/purpose/vision
With painful clarity she remembered the day he had died.

senses.text: if a picture, substance or sound has clarity, you can see or hear it very clearly, or see through it easily
examples: the clarity of the sound on the recording

senses.text: to aim an attack or a criticism at somebody/something
examples: He accused the group of deliberately targeting civilians.
The company has been targeted by animal rights groups for its use of dogs in drugs trials.
The criticism was targeted chiefly at the managing director.
The missiles were mainly targeted at the United States.

senses.text: to try to have an effect on a particular group of people or particular thing
examples: Film-makers are increasingly targeting international markets.
The campaign is specifically targeted at children.
This hospital has been targeted for additional funding.

senses.text: the feeling of being frustrated
examples: Dave thumped the table in frustration.
She couldn't stand the frustration of not being able to help.
sexual frustration

senses.text: something that causes you to feel frustrated
examples: Every job has its difficulties and frustrations.
She took out her frustrations on the children.
My biggest frustration was not having enough time.

senses.text: to raise somebody/something or be raised to a higher position or level
examples: I lifted the lid of the box and peered in.
She lifted her head to look at him.
He lifted the toddler up and cuddled her.
He stood there with his arms lifted above his head.
John lifted his eyes (= looked up) from his book.

senses.text: to take hold of somebody/something and move them/it to a different position
examples: I lifted the baby out of the chair.
He lifted the suitcase down from the rack.

senses.text: a person who hates somebody or who acts or speaks against somebody/something
examples: She didn't have an enemy in the world.
He has a lot of enemies in the company.
After just one day, she had already made an enemy of her manager.
They united in the face of a common enemy.
They used to be friends but they are now sworn enemies (= are determined not to become friends again).

senses.text: a country or group that you are fighting a war against; the soldiers, etc. of this country or group
examples: The enemy was/were forced to retreat.
to fight/defeat the enemy
enemy forces/combatants
The men came under enemy fire.
They were dropped by parachute behind enemy lines (= in the area controlled by the enemy).

senses.text: the act of taking part in something or dealing with somebody
examples: US involvement in European wars
When she was promoted, she missed the day-to-day involvement with customers.

senses.text: the act of giving a lot of time and attention to something you care about
examples: The new album came out of her growing involvement with contemporary music.
the involvement of parents in their children's education

senses.text: a close relationship with somebody, especially a romantic or sexual relationship with somebody that you are not married to
examples: He spoke openly about his involvement with the singer.
Nurses usually try to avoid emotional involvement with patients.
Her husband's involvement with another woman led to their divorce.

senses.text: a place where children go to be educated
examples: My brother and I went to the same school.
Which school do they attend?
I'm going to the school today to talk to Kim's teacher.
We need more money for roads, hospitals and schools.
The charity runs projects at local schools and youth organizations.

senses.text: the process of learning in a school; the time during your life when you go to a school
examples: to start/leave school
to start/quit school
Where did you go to school?
All my kids are still at school.
Can you remember your first day at school?

senses.text: the time during the day when children are working in a school
examples: School begins at 9.
Shall I meet you after school today?
The kids are at/in school until 3.30.
I'm off school this week.
His mum kept him off school for two weeks when he was ill.

senses.text: a place where people go to learn a particular subject or skill
examples: a drama/language/riding school
He runs a karate school in San Jose, California.

senses.text: a barrier like a door that is used to close an opening in a fence or a wall outside a building
examples: the front/main gate
the back/side gate
an iron gate
to open/close/shut a gate
He pushed open the garden gate.

senses.text: an opening that can be closed by a gate or gates
examples: I was slightly nervous as I entered the gates.
We drove through the palace gates.

senses.text: a way out of an airport through which passengers go to get on their plane
examples: Passengers for Paris should proceed to gate 8.
BA flight 726 to Paris is now boarding at gate 16.

senses.text: not done, seen, happening, etc. very often
examples: a rare disease/occurrence/event
This weekend, visitors will get a rare chance to visit the private apartments.
It's extremely rare for it to be this hot in April.
It is rare to find such loyalty these days.
On the rare occasions when they met he hardly even dared speak to her.

senses.text: existing only in small numbers and therefore valuable or interesting
examples: a rare book
a rare coin/stamp
a rare plant/bird/animal
This species is extremely rare.

senses.text: a written or spoken account of an event, especially one that is published or broadcast
examples: Are these news reports true?
media/press/newspaper reports
And now over to Jim Muir, for a report on the South African election.
A local news station aired a special report on the controversy.
According to this evening's weather report, there will be snow tomorrow.

senses.text: a spoken or written description of something containing information that somebody needs to have
examples: a police report
The company has just released its annual report.
Can you give us a progress report?
You need to compile a report on your findings.

senses.text: an official document written by a group of people who have examined a particular situation or problem
examples: to release/issue a report
The committee will publish its report on the health service next week.
According to the report, we are facing an obesity crisis.

senses.text: annoyed by somebody/something, especially because you have to wait for a long time
examples: I'd been waiting for twenty minutes and I was getting impatient.
The children were growing impatient.
Try not to be too impatient with her.
Sarah was becoming increasingly impatient at their lack of interest.
An impatient driver behind me sounded his horn.

senses.text: wanting to do something soon; wanting something to happen soon
examples: She was clearly impatient to leave.
impatient for change

senses.text: acceptable and appropriate in a particular situation
examples: a fair deal/wage/price
The punishment was very fair.
In the end, a draw was a fair result.
I give you fair warning, I’m not always this generous.
We wanted to resolve this matter in a way that would be fair to her.

senses.text: treating everyone equally and according to the rules or law
examples: She has always been scrupulously fair.
They are fair and decent employers.
demands for a fairer distribution of wealth
his vision of a fairer, kinder society
The new tax is fairer than the old system.

senses.text: to make all the arrangements for something to happen or be provided
examples: He helped to organize various events.
to organize a conference/meeting
to organize a protest/demonstration/rally
to organize a workshop/seminar
I'll invite people if you can organize food and drinks.

senses.text: to arrange something or the parts of something into a particular order or structure
examples: Modern computers can organize large amounts of data very quickly.
You should try and organize your time better.
We do not fully understand how the brain is organized.
The complex is organized around a central courtyard.
We need to organize the work according to the availability and skills of each volunteer.

senses.text: to mention or describe something/somebody
examples: She still speaks about him with great affection.
He was speaking publicly for the first time about the incident.
Witnesses spoke of a great ball of flame.
Her students speak very highly of her (= they say very good things about her).
Speaking of travelling, (= referring back to a subject just mentioned) are you going anywhere exciting this year?

senses.text: to make a speech to an audience
examples: Hundreds of people came to hear her speak.
I hate speaking in public.
He was invited to speak at a conference.
to speak on the radio
Professor Todd spoke to the group about her research.

senses.text: an arrangement with a company in which you pay them regular amounts of money and they agree to pay the costs, for example, if you die or are ill, or if you lose or damage something
examples: health/medical insurance
car/travel/unemployment insurance
Private insurance accounts for about 35 per cent of healthcare in the US.
to have/buy insurance
The bill would require employers to provide insurance for their workers.

senses.text: the business of providing people with insurance
examples: an insurance broker/company
the insurance industry/business
He works in insurance.
She's in insurance.

senses.text: a popular style of clothes, hair, etc. at a particular time or place; the state of being popular
examples: the latest fashion trends
the new season’s fashions
Jeans are always in fashion.
Long skirts have come into fashion again.
Some styles never go out of fashion.

senses.text: the business of making or selling clothes in new and different styles
examples: They all want to work in fashion.
household names in the world of fashion and design
a fashion magazine
the fashion industry

senses.text: the process of preparing food by heating it
examples: My husband does all the cooking.
a book on Indian cooking
We always use olive oil for cooking.
Cooking is her passion.

senses.text: food that has been prepared in a particular way
examples: The restaurant offers traditional home cooking (= food similar to that cooked at home).
They serve good French cooking.

senses.text: to become greater in size, number or importance; to make something greater in size, number or importance
examples: Metals expand when they are heated.
Student numbers are expanding rapidly.
a greatly expanded version of his earlier book
A child's vocabulary expands through reading.
The waist expands to fit all sizes.

senses.text: if a business expands or is expanded, new branches are opened, it makes more money, etc.
examples: an expanding economy (= with more businesses starting and growing)
The organic market is rapidly expanding among middle-income shoppers.
They have recently expanded into the Middle East.
We've expanded the business by opening two more stores.
The company is planning to expand its American operations.

senses.text: not affected by something, especially something that you might expect to be harmful
examples: You'll eventually become immune to criticism.
Few people are immune to her charms.
Our business is far from immune to economic conditions.

senses.text: protected from something and therefore able to avoid it
examples: No one should be immune from prosecution.
Not even the monarch was immune from criticism by the press.

senses.text: not easy; needing effort or skill to do or to understand
examples: The competition judges were given a very difficult task.
a difficult decision/question
Asking for help is extremely difficult for some people.
It's really difficult to read your writing.
Your writing is really difficult to read.

senses.text: full of problems; causing a lot of trouble
examples: I found myself in a difficult situation.
difficult conditions/circumstances
I'd had a difficult time and needed a break.
a difficult year/month
My boss is making life very difficult for me.

senses.text: a grade in an exam or for a piece of work at school or university that is very good
a special mark or award given as a reward for good behaviour at school
examples: Other schools already have merit systems in place that reward good behaviour and attitudes.

senses.text: something that you allow or do, or allow somebody to have, in order to end an argument or to make a situation less difficult
examples: The firm will be forced to make concessions if it wants to avoid a strike.
to win a concession from somebody
a major/an important concession
She made no concession to his inexperience as a manager.

senses.text: the act of giving something or allowing something; the act of conceding
examples: the concession of university status to some colleges
her concession speech (= when she admitted that she had lost the election)
Military support was offered in return for the concession of territory.

senses.text: a deep round dish with a wide open top, used especially for holding food or liquid
examples: a salad/fruit/sugar bowl
a washing-up bowl
I refilled the dog's water bowl.
Sieve the flour into a bowl.

senses.text: the amount contained in a bowl
examples: He poured himself a bowl of soup.
I ate a bowl of cereal.
a bowl of rice/noodles/porridge/water

senses.text: to damage or destroy something deliberately to prevent an enemy from using it or to protest about something
examples: The main electricity supply had been sabotaged by the rebels.

senses.text: to prevent something from being successful or being achieved, especially deliberately
examples: Protesters failed to sabotage the peace talks.
The rise in interest rates sabotaged any chance of the firm's recovery.
They had tried to sabotage our plans.
His speech was calculated to sabotage our efforts to reach a solution to the crisis.

senses.text: to examine something to see if it is correct, safe or acceptable
examples: She gave me the minutes of the meeting to read and check.
Check the oil and water before setting off.
Check your work before handing it in.
Customs officers have the right to check all luggage going through customs.
The cars were checked to see whether the faults had been spotted.

senses.text: to look at something or ask somebody to find out if something/somebody is present, correct or true or if something is how you think it is
examples: We'll check back in a couple of hours.
We were interrupted by a waiter checking to see if everything was OK.
‘Is Mary in the office?’ ‘Just a moment. I'll go and check.’
Hang on—I just need to check my email.
She's constantly checking her phone—it drives me mad.

senses.text: a conversation about somebody/something; the process of discussing somebody/something
examples: I discovered something interesting during a family discussion.
We'll continue this discussion some other time.
We had a discussion with them about the differences between Britain and the US.
A lively discussion followed about whether he should be allowed to join the club.
The pair held informal discussions on a number of topics.

senses.text: a speech or a piece of writing that discusses many different aspects of a subject
examples: I have chosen to focus my discussion on a single work.
Her article is a discussion of the methods used in research.
See chapter 15 for a detailed discussion of the approach used.

senses.text: useful, easy or quick to do; not causing problems
examples: Fruit is a convenient source of vitamins and energy.
A bicycle is often more convenient than a car in towns.
I can't see him now—it isn't convenient.
I'll call back at a more convenient time.
For now, the most convenient way to access the internet remains the smartphone.

senses.text: near to a particular place; easy to get to
examples: The office is just five minutes from the station, so it's very convenient.
The house is very convenient for several schools.
The house is quite convenient for the shops.
The hotel is convenient to downtown.

senses.text: to provide yourself/somebody/something with the things that are needed for a particular purpose or activity
examples: to be fully/poorly equipped
She got a bank loan to rent and equip a small workshop.
He equipped himself with a street plan.
The centre is well equipped for canoeing and mountaineering.
Rooms vary in size and come equipped with hairdryers, television and telephone.

senses.text: to prepare somebody for an activity or task, especially by teaching them what they need to know
examples: The course is designed to equip students for a career in nursing.
Here he received the education that would equip him to take his place in society.

senses.text: to try to reach an agreement by formal discussion
examples: The government will not negotiate with terrorists.
We have been negotiating for more pay.
They have refused to negotiate on this issue.
a strong negotiating position
negotiating skills

senses.text: to arrange or agree something by formal discussion
examples: to negotiate a deal/contract/treaty/settlement
We successfully negotiated the release of the hostages.

senses.text: a type or variety of something
examples: all the millions of different life forms on the planet today
forms of transport/government/entertainment
one of the most common forms of cancer
Swimming is one of the best forms of exercise.
Along with talking and singing, drawing must be the oldest form of communication.

senses.text: a way of writing or saying a word that shows, for example, if it is plural or in a particular tense
examples: the infinitive form of the verb

senses.text: the fact of leaving your job and stopping work, usually because you have reached a particular age; the time when you do this
examples: At 60, he was now approaching retirement.
Susan is going to take early retirement (= retire before the usual age).
retirement age
This year we have seen the retirements of several senior personnel.
a retirement pension

senses.text: the period of your life after you have stopped work, usually because you have reached a particular age
examples: He provided for a comfortable retirement by selling the business.
We all wish you a long and happy retirement.
Up to a third of one's life is now being spent in retirement.

senses.text: to tell somebody something that you have heard or been told by somebody else
examples: I don't want you to repeat a word of this to anyone.
The rumour has been widely repeated in the press.

senses.text: to happen more than once in the same way
examples: History has a strange way of repeating itself.
This phrase is repeated at intervals throughout the song.
a repeating pattern/design
A single note repeated over and over again, throbbing in my head.

senses.text: to plan and direct the course of ship, plane, car etc., for example by using a map
examples: to navigate by the stars
I'll drive, and you can navigate.
There's nothing worse than navigating through heavy traffic.
How do you navigate your way through a forest?

senses.text: to sail along, over or through a sea, river etc.
examples: The river became too narrow and shallow to navigate.
Only the best-trained captains could safely navigate these routes.

senses.text: a change that makes something more accurate than it was before
examples: I've made a few small corrections to your report.
The paper had to publish a correction to the story.

senses.text: the act or process of correcting something
examples: There are some programming errors that need correction.

senses.text: having a desire to cause physical or mental pain and make somebody suffer
examples: He was known to be a cruel dictator.
I can't stand people who are cruel to animals.
Her eyes were cruel and hard.
Sometimes you have to be cruel to be kind (= make somebody suffer because it will be good for them later).
an extremely cruel regime

senses.text: causing physical or mental pain and making somebody suffer
examples: a cruel joke/hoax
cruel punishment
It was a cruel irony that he, being gravely ill, would survive his family.
It would be a cruel twist of fate if he escaped only to starve to death once outside.
Her father's death was a cruel blow.

senses.text: the production of goods from raw materials, especially in factories
examples: the needs of British industry
the Department of Trade and Industry
She got a job in industry.

senses.text: the people and activities involved in producing a particular thing, or in providing a particular service
examples: the steel/oil/auto industry
the music/film/tourism industry
We need to develop local industries.
She later worked in the banking industry.
The whole issue has raised concern within the food industry.

senses.text: a large amount of a substance that does not have a definite shape or form
examples: She saw a large dark mass in the water.
The hill appeared as a black mass in the distance.
a mass of snow and rocks falling down the mountain
The sky was full of dark masses of clouds.

senses.text: a large amount or quantity of something
examples: a mass of blonde hair
I began sifting through the mass of evidence.
Both titles give readers a mass of information.

senses.text: a large number of people or things grouped together, often in a confused way
examples: I struggled through the mass of people to the exit.
The page was covered with a mass of figures.
a chaotic mass of ideas

senses.text: a large number or amount of something
examples: There were masses of people in the shops yesterday.
I've got masses of work to do.
Don't give me any more. I've eaten masses!
There is masses of cake left over from the party.

senses.text: the ordinary people in society
examples: government attempts to suppress dissatisfaction among the masses
a TV programme that brings science to the masses

senses.text: a period of time when somebody/something has to wait because of a problem that makes something slow or late
examples: Commuters will face long delays on the roads today.
to experience/suffer delays
to avoid/minimize further delays
Increased congestion and traffic delays are expected.
a delay of two hours/a two-hour delay

senses.text: failure to do something quickly or at the right time; the act of delaying
examples: There's no time for delay.
Report it to the police without delay (= immediately).

senses.text: to make something or somebody ready to be used or to do something
examples: to prepare a report
A hotel room is being prepared for them.
The college prepares students for a career in business.
The training has prepared me to deal with any medical issue.
We all set about preparing for the party.

senses.text: to make yourself ready to do something or for something that you expect to happen
examples: I had no time to prepare.
The whole class is working hard preparing for the exams.
The police are preparing themselves for trouble at the demonstration.
I had been preparing myself for this moment.
Her condition is not improving as we hoped. You must prepare yourselves for the worst.

senses.text: to make food ready to be eaten
examples: He was in the kitchen preparing lunch.
to prepare meals/food/dinner

senses.text: not modern; no longer fashionable
examples: old-fashioned clothes/styles/methods/equipment
These sweets are still made in the old-fashioned way.
The Winnedumah Hotel has old-fashioned charm.
His clothes were curiously old-fashioned.
Critics regarded her films as hopelessly old-fashioned.

senses.text: believing in old or traditional ways; having traditional ideas
examples: My parents are old-fashioned about relationships and marriage.
She wants to be an old-fashioned mother, staying at home and looking after the children.
Call me old-fashioned, but I still believe in good manners.
She's somewhat old-fashioned in her attitudes.
He seemed too old-fashioned and formal.

senses.text: the feeling of enjoying yourself; activities that you enjoy
examples: We had a lot of fun at Sarah's party.
Sailing is good fun.
It was great fun! You should have come too.
That was the most fun I have had in years.
Have fun! (= Enjoy yourself)

senses.text: behaviour or activities that are not serious but are meant to be enjoyed
examples: She's very lively and full of fun.
We didn't mean to hurt him. It was just a bit of fun.
You have to have a sense of fun to be a good teacher.
It wasn't serious—it was all done in fun.

senses.text: the rate at which something happens or is repeated
examples: Fatal road accidents have decreased in frequency over recent years.
Dawn was then visiting New York with increasing frequency.
a society with a high/low frequency (= happening often/not very often) of stable marriages
Users will be able to rearrange their icons by frequency of use.
The program can show us word frequency (= how often words occur in a language).

senses.text: the fact of something happening often
examples: the alarming frequency of computer errors
The relative frequency of this illness in the area is of concern to all doctors.
Objects like this turn up at sales with surprising frequency.
Bullets bounced off the rock with alarming frequency.

senses.text: a particular length of time
examples: a long/an extended period
a short/brief period
a period of transition/uncertainty/expansion
a period of two years/six months/four weeks
a two-year/six-month/four-week period

senses.text: a length of time in the life of a particular person or in the history of a particular country
examples: Which period of history would you most like to have lived in?
This textbook covers the post-war period.
The church dates from the Norman period.
We are currently studying the early medieval period.
Like Picasso, she too had a blue period.

senses.text: a university course that is of a higher level than a basic course (in the US also used to describe a class in school that is at a higher level than other classes)
examples: an honours degree/course
a First Class Honours degree
I took an honors class in English.

senses.text: if you pass an exam or graduate from a university or school with honours, you receive a special grade for having achieved a very high standard
examples: He passed with second-class honours.

senses.text: to affect or make something affect, be known by, or be used by more and more people
examples: The news had spread and was causing great excitement.
Use of computers spread rapidly during that period.
Within weeks, his confidence had spread throughout the team.
The disease spreads easily.
The idea spread quickly in the years that followed.

senses.text: to cover, or to make something cover, a larger and larger area
examples: There is no evidence that the cancer has spread.
The fire rapidly spread to adjoining buildings.
Water began to spread across the floor.
A smile spread slowly across her face.
Allow plenty of space for this plant as its roots spread widely.

senses.text: to cause somebody/something to be in a number of different places
examples: Seeds and pollen are spread by the wind.
We have 10 000 members spread all over the country.

senses.text: the use of the imagination to express ideas or feelings, particularly in painting, drawing or sculpture
examples: modern/contemporary art
an art critic/historian/lover
Can we call television art?
stolen works of art
Her performance displayed great art.

senses.text: examples of objects such as paintings, drawings or sculptures
examples: an art gallery/exhibition
a collection of art and antiques

senses.text: the skill of creating objects such as paintings and drawings, especially when you study it
examples: She's good at art and design.
He has a master's degree in fine art.
an art teacher/student/college/class

senses.text: feelings of anger and hate that may result in threatening or violent behaviour
examples: Video games have been blamed for causing aggression.
Nowadays our aggression is channelled into sports.

senses.text: a violent attack or threats by one person against another person or by one country against another country
examples: unprovoked military aggression

senses.text: behaving in a sensible way, like an adult
examples: Jane is very mature for her age.
a mature and sensible attitude
She tries to look mature and sophisticated.

senses.text: fully grown and developed
examples: sexually mature
a mature oak/eagle/elephant

senses.text: that can be trusted to do something well; that you can rely on
examples: We are looking for someone who is reliable and hard-working.
a reliable friend
He was a very reliable and honest man who would never betray anyone.

senses.text: that is likely to be correct or true
examples: reliable information/data
Our information comes from a reliable source.
Prosecution lawyers tried to show that she was not a reliable witness.
How reliable are the local weather forecasts?
These tests are a reliable indicator of future performance.

senses.text: an understanding of what something is like
examples: There are many valuable insights in her book.
His work offers several useful insights.
The book gives us fascinating insights into life in Mexico.
I hope you have gained some insight into the difficulties we face.
She has some interesting insights on the principles that have guided US foreign policy.

senses.text: the ability to see and understand the truth about people or situations
examples: He has insight, vision and a deep humanity.
She was a writer of great insight.
With a flash of insight I realized what the dream meant.

senses.text: tending to destroy something slowly by chemical action
examples: the corrosive effects of salt water
corrosive acid

senses.text: tending to damage something gradually
examples: Unemployment is having a corrosive effect on our economy.

senses.text: fun, attractive, or giving pleasure
examples: a pleasant evening/atmosphere/walk
a pleasant aroma/smell/scent
What a pleasant surprise!
to live in pleasant surroundings
music that is pleasant to the ear

senses.text: friendly and polite
examples: a pleasant young man
a pleasant smile/voice/manner
Please try to be pleasant to our guests.
She was perfectly pleasant, though a little cool.

senses.text: formal permission given by a company to somebody who wants to sell its goods or services in a particular area; formal permission given by a government to somebody who wants to operate a public service as a business
examples: a franchise agreement/company
a catering/rail franchise
In the reorganization, Southern Television lost their franchise.
to operate a business under franchise

senses.text: a business or service run under franchise
examples: They operate franchises in London and Paris.
He's got a job as manager of a burger franchise.
He runs a local pizza franchise.

senses.text: something that is difficult to understand or to explain
examples: It is one of the great unsolved mysteries of this century.
Their motives remain a mystery.
She tried to solve the mystery of the boy's sudden death.
It's a complete mystery to me why they chose him.

senses.text: a person or thing that is strange and interesting because you do not know much about them or it
examples: He's a bit of a mystery.
There was a mystery guest on the programme.
The mystery man's identity remains hidden.
a mystery disease/illness/virus
a mystery tour (= when you do not know where you are going)

senses.text: the quality of being difficult to understand or to explain, especially when this makes somebody/something seem interesting and exciting
examples: Mystery surrounds her disappearance.
His past is shrouded in mystery (= not much is known about it).
The dark glasses give her an air of mystery.
She’s a woman of mystery.

senses.text: a story, a film or a play in which crimes and strange events are only explained at the end
examples: He is the author of several murder mysteries.
The film is based on Daphne DuMaurier's mystery novel, ‘Rebecca’.

senses.text: a long seat for two or more people, usually made of wood
examples: a park bench

senses.text: the seats where players sit when they are not playing in the game
examples: the substitutes’ bench
He started the game on the bench but finished as the team’s top scorer.
He's sick of spending every game on the bench.

senses.text: to obey a rule, law, etc.
examples: The building does not conform with safety regulations.

senses.text: to agree with or match something
examples: It did not conform to the usual stereotype of an industrial city.
Wordsworth changed the ideas of his poem to conform with his later religious and political opinions.
the attempt to force science to conform with a political or social ideology

senses.text: to hit something hard, often by accident
examples: Be careful you don't knock your head on this low beam.
Her hand knocked against the glass.
She dropped the pile of books when he accidentally knocked her shoulder.

senses.text: to hit something so that it moves or breaks
examples: He'd knocked over a glass of water.
I knocked the nail into the wall.
They had to knock the door down to get in.
He had knocked one of the pictures off the wall.
The boys were knocking (= kicking) a ball around in the back yard.

senses.text: something that helps or encourages somebody/something
examples: a great/tremendous/welcome boost
The tax cuts will give a much needed boost to the economy.
Winning the competition was a wonderful boost for her morale.

senses.text: an increase in something
examples: a boost in car sales
a big boost in exports

senses.text: a detailed drawing of a machine, building, etc. that shows its size, shape and measurements
examples: The original plans of the building have not survived.
The architect is drawing up plans for the new offices.
Plans for our extension have been submitted for approval.
A final version of the plans and specifications has been produced.

senses.text: a diagram that shows how something will be arranged
examples: a seating plan (= showing where each person will sit, for example at a dinner)
a floor plan (= showing how furniture is arranged)

senses.text: fine soft thread produced by silkworms
examples: The caterpillar spins the silk around its entire body.

senses.text: a type of fine smooth cloth made from silk thread; a piece of this cloth
examples: a silk dress/blouse/scarf/tie/sari
silk stockings
made of pure silk
Her skin was as smooth as silk.
fine silks produced in Italy

senses.text: improving only the outside appearance of something and not its basic character
examples: These reforms are not merely cosmetic.
She dismissed the plan as a cosmetic exercise to win votes.

senses.text: connected with medical treatment that is intended to improve a person’s appearance
examples: cosmetic surgery
cosmetic dental work

senses.text: a reason, either true or invented, that you give to explain or defend your behaviour
examples: Late again! What's your excuse this time?
I tried desperately to think of a good excuse.
a lame/feeble/poor/flimsy excuse
a reasonable/valid excuse
I know I missed the deadline, but I have an excuse.

senses.text: a good reason that you give for doing something that you want to do for other reasons
examples: Eating ice cream for a sore throat isn't really a cure, but I like it, so any excuse!
It's just an excuse for a party.
Any occasion is a good excuse for having a barbecue.
It gave me an excuse to take the car.
He tried unsuccessfully to find an excuse to leave.

senses.text: all the events that happened in the past
examples: The battle was a turning point in human history.
one of the worst disasters in recent history
Many people throughout history have dreamt of a world without war.
a people with no sense of history
The area was inhabited long before the dawn of recorded history (= before people wrote about events).

senses.text: the past events connected with the development of a particular place, subject, etc.
examples: the history of science/music/philosophy
She is an expert in the history of the United States.
The local history of the area is fascinating.
The museum traces the history of chocolate.

senses.text: the study of past events, especially as a subject at school or university
examples: a history professor/teacher
a history lesson/textbook
a degree in History
cultural/social/economic/political history
modern/ancient history

senses.text: following a style that is popular at a particular time
examples: fashionable clothes/ideas/styles
He was laughed at by his more fashionable friends.
It's becoming fashionable to have long hair again.
Such thinking is currently fashionable among right-wing politicians.

senses.text: used or visited by people following a current fashion, especially by rich people
examples: a fashionable address/resort/restaurant
She lives in a very fashionable part of London.
The shop moved to a more fashionable address.
The play is a satire on fashionable society in Victorian times.

senses.text: connected with or about love or a sexual relationship
examples: a romantic candlelit dinner
a romantic comedy
romantic stories/fiction
I'm not interested in a romantic relationship.
It wasn't even until a hundred or so years ago that the concept of romantic love in marriage gained any real popularity.

senses.text: showing feelings of love
examples: Why don't you ever give me flowers? I wish you'd be more romantic.
You're getting quite romantic in your old age!
I'm hopelessly romantic and dreamy.

senses.text: beautiful in a way that makes you think of love or feel strong emotions
examples: romantic music
romantic mountain scenery
romantic images of deserted beaches
It sounded romantic and exciting to work in the Walled City, but few stuck it more than a few weeks.

senses.text: the greatest or smallest amount of something that is allowed
examples: They imposed a strict spending limit.
His speed was double the legal limit.
Do not exceed the recommended limit of 6g of salt per day.
The EU has set strict limits on levels of pollution.
to keep government spending within acceptable limits

senses.text: a point at which something stops being possible or existing
examples: She knew the limits of her power.
to set/define the limits of something
to push/test the limits of something
It's a movie that stretches the limits of believability.
His arrogance knew (= had) no limits.

senses.text: to suggest that something is true or that you feel or think something, without saying so directly
examples: Are you implying (that) I am wrong?
His silence seemed to imply agreement.
High-yield bonds, as the name implies, offer a high rate of interest.
It was implied that we were at fault.

senses.text: to make it seem likely that something is true or exists
examples: The survey implies (that) more people are moving house than was thought.
It was implied in the survey that…
Popularity does not necessarily imply merit.
The fact that she was here implies a degree of interest.

senses.text: forming a unit by itself; not joined to something else
examples: They have begun to sleep in separate rooms.
Raw meat must be kept separate from cooked meat.
The school is housed in two separate buildings.
Write a list of names on a separate piece of paper.

senses.text: different; not connected
examples: a separate incident/issue
The companies now exist as two separate entities.
I try to keep my private life separate from my work.
This is a completely separate matter.
It happened on three separate occasions.

senses.text: a competition in which people try to win something
examples: to hold a singing/talent contest
to enter/win a contest
She was awarded the first prize in an essay contest .
It was a close contest between two evenly matched crews.
He said he had enjoyed the contest with his rival for the title .

senses.text: a struggle to gain control or power
examples: the leadership/presidential contest
Both parties are predicting victory in this close contest.
a contest for the leadership of the party
a contest between traditional and liberal views
It looks like being a close-fought contest with the Conservatives.
